Patch Set 6:

(5 comments)

Patch Set 6: Code-Review-1

This needs to skip sanity test_151/test_156 when running with cache disabled, or is that just because the patch permanently disables read cache and ignores the /proc settings?

I think this patch should be updated to only conditionally enable the non-cache IO and fortestonly removed, and see whether it passes.  Then we can do performance testing more easily once it is landed.

Patch Set 6:

Also, sanity test_255b needs to be skipped if cache is disabled.

Patch Set 6:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 6: Code-Review-1

holding a pages in ost thread info contantly is bad idea.
typical OST have a 512-720 threads (due CPT partitions some can don't used),
so up 1k ost_io threads. 
1K * 16M = 16G
so this memory was removed from system use but can don't used long time.
from other side - number is osd thread env can much bigger a number of ost_io threads,
so using a thread local storage as RNB will be better place with ability to shrink this.

Patch Set 6:

> from other side - number is osd thread env can much bigger a number of ost_io threads, so using a thread local storage as RNB will be better place with ability to shrink this.

I don't think this is correct as pointers/pages are allocated on demand, not in every osd_thread_info.

Patch Set 6:

Alex,

I haven't read the patch to understand in detail (sorry, I plan to), but if they're allocated as needed, I see two possible ways to do that:

Allocate and free every time (I would think that's unacceptable for performance) and allocate and keep forever, which in that case, has the same memory usage problem Alexey described if we actually use the osd threads.  (Which hopefully we will, otherwise why are they there?)

I think we need some sort of pool to pull from, which I believe is what he's suggesting...?

Patch Set 6:

we used the schema proposed before pagecache support, it was statically allocated pages for every ost_io thread.

Patch Set 6:

Patrick, this is how Lustre worked exclusively until 1.8, until the OSS read cache was implemented - a dedicated per-thread RDMA page list. This can reduce CPU overhead significantly. Since the read cache is disabled in this case, the RAM on the OSS will be much less used with per-thread page lists than the data cache. 

If you prefer to tune the system to have a huge number of threads, then you don't have to enable this feature.

Alex, my suggestion is to check nonrotational at startup, and use that to set the cache behavior. Setting a parameter afterward will override the default.
Uploaded patch set 7.

Patch Set 7:

Looks good to me.

Patch Set 7:

Andreas, so I changed that patch that it disables cache on start if it's backed by non-rotational storage. but that would mean one needs to enable cache somehow on every start?

Patch Set 7:

Code-wise looks good to me.

Patch Set 9: Code-Review-1

LU env allocated / freed on each request (via refill), while ptlrpc TLS is fill once.
Sometimes, LU env count is more than number of OST IO threads.

Patch Set 9:

> LU env allocated / freed on each request (via refill), while ptlrpc TLS is fill once.

this is incorrect statement. 

LU env is allocated once per thread - see ptlrpc_main()
refill just adds missing "keys", once added nothing is need to be allocated in a given env.

Patch Set 10:

I think you could add couple printk() to verify your idea. I did.

Patch Set 10:

(2 comments)

Patrick, one reason I'd be in favor of changing the default behavior for non-rotational storage is if this noticeably improves performance over the unpatched code.

While it is possible to say that there can be a tunable that is selected by some knowledgeable admin, I'd argue that there are already many tunables that the admin doesn't understand, and the end result is that only a very few sites (if any) would every change it from the default.

I'm not advocating changing it for every OST, but if after testing this shows itself to be a win on flash storage, why wouldn't we want to help users of flash storage to get good behavior?  Of course, if you show me that this is a net loss for some flash devices (USB sticks notwithstanding) then I'd agree my heuristic is flawed, and try to look for a better one (e.g. only NVMe devices or similar, and not SATA SSDs).

Patch Set 10:

Andreas,

I could find such examples, but they would just be page cache dependent workloads, where data is used from memory.  Straightforward enough and an obvious tradeoff here.

My basic point is that the issue (as I understand it) with the page cache on servers is throughput, not latency.  Lustre is too slow (in the sense of latency) for page cache latency to matter when accessing flash.

But I am not opposed to changing the default, particularly because OSTs are only getting faster, and flash OSTs are likely to be faster than disk based OSTs.

Patch Set 10:

Reading your post again, I should stress/clarify a bit:
Since the issue is throughput, not latency, the tech used doesn't matter much.  It's possible to have these issues with any (non-USB stick ;) ) type of backing media if you have enough of it used for one OST.

But short of benchmarking on startup, there's no easy heuristic for *total* throughput.  And flash is, all else being equal, probably going to be faster than disk...

Patch Set 12:

(2 comments)

This strikes me as somewhat inefficient. Why not allocate the max buffer in one go? Even better if we preallocate it on module insertion.

Otherwise some choppy io at the beginning would lead to a very fragmented buffer with no way to clear it.

Patch Set 12:

(1 comment)

> Why not allocate the max buffer in one go?
 > Even better if we preallocate it on module insertion.

Allocating all of the memory at module insertion is potentially wasteful.  We may not even use these buffers unless the storage is on flash, or it is enabled by the admin (off by default), so that would be up to 16MB * num_threads, or 4GB by default, that might be wasted.

Patch Set 12:

(1 comment)

> (1 comment)
 > 
 > > Why not allocate the max buffer in one go?
 > > Even better if we preallocate it on module insertion.
 > 
 > Allocating all of the memory at module insertion is potentially
 > wasteful.  We may not even use these buffers unless the storage is
 > on flash, or it is enabled by the admin (off by default), so that
 > would be up to 16MB * num_threads, or 4GB by default, that might be
 > wasted.

Well, we did this before (in 1.8) and it was ok. We can make it optional so that users that do not have ssds/do not want to use this are not affected and the users that do will need it anyway and so it's not really wasteful then.

After all if this is requested - we are practically guaranteed every thread will serve one request like this and will do those allocations anyway.

Patch Set 12:

(1 comment)

Patch Set 12: Code-Review+2

I don't quite agree about on demand, at least we should do in in larger chunks, say 1 megabyte at a time or such.
I am letting this land since the improvement is still there, but please follow it up with the less fragmented allocation logic.
Change has been successfully cherry-picked as 72372486a5e96dd2078e01a697025da41bbf03c2 by Oleg Drokin

parents.subject: LU-11199 mdt: Attempt lookup lock on open

commit.subject: LU-11347 osd: do not use pagecache for I/O
commit.message:
LU-11347 osd: do not use pagecache for I/O

for testing purposes cache is constantly disabled.

 - with non-rotational storage
 - when both read and write caches are disable
 - sanityn/16c to run fsx with cache disable
